description: The displayed image is a software interface button labeled 'Check for updates', which has no correlation with the provided low-density steel research paper content. It consists of a circular ring (upper segment blue, lower segment yellow) with a red bookmark-shaped icon at its center, placed on a light gray square background. No material-related characterization results, sample information, mechanical property data, or microstructure details relevant to the Fe-0.25C-3Mn-2Al-0.4Si low-density steel study can be obtained from this irrelevant image, which does not contain any content related to the material preparation, microstructure characterization, or performance testing mentioned in the associated paper.

This group of figures presents the fabrication process of Fe-0.25C-3Mn-2Al low-density steel and the sampling method for mechanical testing. The first subfigure is a temperature-time process schematic, detailing thermomechanical treatment parameters: holding at 1200 °C for 60 min, hot rolling starting at 1100 °C, finishing rolling at 880 °C, followed by cold rolling, then intercritical annealing at 750/800/850 °C (between Ac1 and Ac3 critical points) for 30 min, with optional subsequent aging at 400 °C for 30 min. Micrographs of microstructures corresponding to each process stage are also attached. The second subfigure shows the 1.6 mm thick as-cold-rolled thin-wall steel plate (with rolling direction RD marked), and dimension parameters of the tensile specimen machined from the plate: total length 58 mm, gauge length 14 mm, gauge width 5 mm, gauge thickness 1 mm, clamping end width 10 mm. This figure supports subsequent microstructure regulation and mechanical property investigation of the developed low-cost high-performance low-density steel.

This set of scanning electron microscopy images illustrates the multiphase microstructure evolution of Fe-0.25C-3Mn-2Al-0.4Si low-density steel under different thermomechanical processing and heat treatment conditions. Subfigures (a) and (b) show as-hot rolled (HR) and as-cold rolled microstructures respectively, dominated by lath-shaped bainite (B) and fresh martensite (FM) with 10 μm scale bars, while the high-magnification inset of (b) and subfigures (c-h) use 5 μm scale bars. Subfigures (c-e) correspond to samples intercritically annealed at 750℃ (AN750), 800℃ (AN800), 850℃ (AN850) for 30 min, composed of ferrite (F), B, FM, and retained austenite (RA), with RA content decreasing as annealing temperature rises. Subfigures (f-h) show samples after additional 400℃ aging for 30 min (AN750-T, AN800-T, AN850-T), where tempered martensite (TM) is formed. This figure confirms that controlled rolling, intercritical annealing and aging can tailor heterogeneous multiphase microstructures to achieve excellent strength-ductility balance in the low-density steel.

This figure characterizes retained austenite (RA) evolution in Fe-0.25C-3Mn-2Al-0.4Si low-density steel under different thermomechanical processing conditions. Subplots (a)-(d) are EBSD phase maps for hot-rolled (HR), 750℃ annealed (AN750), 800℃ annealed (AN800), and 850℃ annealed (AN850) samples respectively: green denotes FCC RA, red denotes BCC matrix phases. RA fraction is 0 vol% for HR, 3.0 vol% (average size 0.59 μm) for AN750, 1.1 vol% (average size 0.72 μm) for AN800, 0.2 vol% (average size 0.83 μm) for AN850. Subplot (e) is a statistical plot summarizing RA content trend, which peaks at AN750 and decreases with higher annealing temperature, while average RA size increases with annealing temperature. Subplot (f) shows XRD patterns identifying α (BCC) and γ (FCC) phases, with γ peak intensity consistent with the RA content trend, confirming intercritical annealing at 750℃ yields the highest RA content for the studied steel.

This figure presents characterization results of Fe-0.25C-3Mn-2Al low-density steel samples (AN750-T, AN800-T, AN850-T, produced via intercritical annealing at 750 °C, 800 °C, 850 °C respectively for 30 min followed by air cooling, then aging at 400 °C for 30 min) using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), selected area electron diffraction (SAED), and X-ray diffraction (XRD) for microstructure and phase identification. 10 subfigures are TEM bright-field and dark-field micrographs with scales from 100 nm to 2 μm, distinguishing phases: equiaxed ferrite (F), lath-shaped bainite (B), tempered martensite (TM), island-like fresh martensite (FM), and nanoscale film-like retained austenite (RA). A SAED pattern indexed to the [110] zone axis of FCC γ-austenite confirms RA identity. The XRD plot shows BCC α (ferrite/bainite/martensite) and FCC γ (austenite) peaks, with γ peak intensity decreasing as annealing temperature rises, matching measured RA contents of 7.4% (AN750-T), 2.4% (AN800-T), 0.6% (AN850-T). TEM differentiates B (carbides at 60° to lath boundaries) from TM (randomly distributed carbides, indistinguishable via EBSD). The figure confirms the multiphase heterogeneous microstructure of the studied steels, and the trend of reduced RA content with increasing annealing temperature.

This figure presents electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) characterization for phase separation and interphase strain analysis of Fe-0.25C-3Mn-2Al-0.4Si low-density steel samples AN750-T, AN800-T, AN850-T, which are intercritically annealed at 750 °C, 800 °C, 850 °C respectively then aged at 400 °C. The first row shows combined phase and image quality (IQ) maps: green represents FCC phase (retained austenite, RA) and red represents BCC phases. RA volume fractions decrease with increasing annealing temperature, being 7.4%, 2.4%, 0.6% for AN750-T, AN800-T, AN850-T respectively. The next row shows bimodal IQ distribution histograms that classify BCC phases into fresh martensite (FM, ~1 μm), bainite/tempered martensite (B/TM), and ferrite (F, ~5 μm). The following rows show Kernel Average Misorientation (KAM) maps (color scale 0-1.25° for misorientation) and KAM line profiles across phase boundaries: KAM peaks at phase boundaries indicate strain concentration from accumulated dislocations. Average KAM values are 0.53° (AN750-T), 0.69° (AN800-T, highest due to highest B/TM+FM content), and 0.62° (AN850-T). This figure validates phase evolution trend and interphase strain concentration behavior that supports heterogeneous deformation strengthening in the studied multiphase steel.

This group of four subplots illustrates microstructure characteristics and mechanical properties of Fe-0.25C-3Mn-2Al-0.4Si low-density steels, treated by intercritical annealing at 750℃, 800℃, 850℃ respectively followed by 400℃ aging (samples AN750-T, AN800-T, AN850-T). The first two bar charts quantify volume fractions and average grain diameters of four constituent phases: ferrite (F), bainite/tempered martensite (B/TM), fresh martensite (FM) and retained austenite (RA). With rising annealing temperature, F volume fraction increases from 49.3% to 54.7%, while FM and RA fractions gradually reduce, with RA dropping to only 0.6% in AN850-T. The engineering stress-strain curves show AN750-T achieves ultimate tensile strength of 1068 MPa and total elongation of 34.0%, while AN800-T and AN850-T have higher tensile strength (~1295 MPa) but lower ductility. The fourth scatter plot compares the developed steels with commercial QP, DP, TRIP, TBF steels, showing the proposed steel reaches a product of strength and elongation (PSE) up to ~36 GPa·%, outperforming most reference steels and reaching the performance level of third-generation advanced high-strength steels. The figure confirms that regulating phase fractions and grain sizes via proper annealing temperature realizes excellent strength-ductility balance.

This set of three line charts presents the volume fraction evolution of deformation-induced martensite (DIM, black ascending curves, left Y-axis, unit: %) and retained austenite (RA, red descending curves, right Y-axis, unit: %) as a function of true strain (X-axis) in Fe-0.25C-3Mn-2Al low-density steel, fitted via the Olson-Cohen (O-C) model for samples with different initial RA contents from different heat treatments. The final DIM fractions at maximum measured true strain are 4.74%, 1.16%, and 0.28% for the three samples, corresponding to gradually decreasing initial RA contents. All subplots show consistent trends: as true strain increases, RA transforms to DIM continuously via the TRIP effect, leading to monotonically increasing DIM fraction and monotonically decreasing RA fraction. These fitting results agree with experimental phase transformation kinetic observations, and support the interpretation of strain hardening and strength-ductility synergy mechanisms of the studied low-density steel.

This figure presents elemental distribution characterization for three aged Fe-0.25C-3Mn-2Al-0.4Si low-density steel samples: AN750-T (annealed at 750 °C + aged at 400 °C), AN800-T (annealed at 800 °C + aged at 400 °C), AN850-T (annealed at 850 °C + aged at 400 °C). The first three subfigures are EPMA elemental mapping images of Fe, C, Mn (marked as M), Al, Si with a 3 μm scale bar, where red dashed arrows indicate the line scan path. The last three subfigures are corresponding line scan plots showing content variations of C, Mn, Al, Si (unit: at%) along 0-30 μm distance, with gray regions marking B/TM (bainite/tempered martensite), RA (retained austenite), FM (fresh martensite) phase zones. C is found to mainly concentrate in B/TM, FM and RA phases, and its distribution becomes more uniform with increasing annealing temperature, while Mn, Al, Si show no obvious distribution differences across samples. This figure supports quantitative phase composition analysis and element partitioning rule study for the multiphase heterogeneous steel.

This set of figures presents true stress-true strain curves from uniaxial tensile tests of three aged Fe-0.25C-3Mn-2Al low-density steel samples (AN750-T, AN800-T, AN850-T), comparing experimental measurements (red solid lines) with prediction results from the iso-work constitutive model (grey dashed lines). The shaded area between the two curves represents back stress (σ_B) induced work hardening, which increases with rising true strain. Inset digital image correlation (DIC) strain field maps show varying strain distribution characteristics: AN750-T exhibits relatively uniform strain and stress relieving during plastic deformation; AN800-T shows secondary local strain concentration that propagates at 45° to cause necking; AN850-T presents relatively uniform strain distribution at early deformation. The maximum σ_B values at necking strain are 217 MPa, 300 MPa, 278 MPa for the three samples, respectively. These results confirm that coordinated deformation between soft and hard domains, back stress hardening, and TRIP effect synergistically improve the ductility of the heterogeneous multiphase steel, mitigating premature failure caused by stress concentration.

This figure combines transmission electron microscopy (TEM) characterization and mechanical property contribution analysis for Fe-0.25C-3Mn-2Al low-density steel samples AN750-T, AN800-T, AN850-T (intercritical annealed at 750/800/850℃ respectively then aged at 400℃). TEM micrographs (bright-field, dark-field) identify multiphase heterogeneous microstructure: soft ferrite (F), hard domains including bainite/tempered martensite (B/TM), fresh martensite (FM), retained austenite (RA), nanoscale carbides inside B/TM, and defects including dislocation pile-ups and dense dislocations at soft-hard phase boundaries, with characteristic size R and heterogeneous layer thickness Δh measured from TEM. Stacked true stress-true strain plots for AN800-T and AN850-T decompose total stress into phase contributions plus back stress σ_B from geometrically necessary dislocation (GND) accumulation at interfaces. Back stress contributes 217/300/278 MPa at necking strain for the three samples, confirming that GND-induced back stress work hardening and coordinated deformation between soft and hard domains deliver excellent strength-ductility balance of the steel.

This group of figures presents scanning electron microscopy (SEM) characterization results of crack propagation behavior, fracture morphology and dimple size distribution for three Fe-0.25C-3Mn-2Al-0.4Si low-density steel samples: AN750-T (intercritically annealed at 750℃ plus 400℃ aging), AN800-T (annealed at 800℃ plus aging) and AN850-T (annealed at 850℃ plus aging). Subfigure (a) shows microcrack initiation and propagation: microvoids first nucleate at soft-hard phase interfaces; for AN750-T and AN800-T, crack tips blunt at hard domain interfaces generating secondary cracks, while hard domains are directly severed in AN850-T. Subfigure (b) shows central fracture zone morphology: AN750-T is dominated by deep dimples, while AN800-T and AN850-T present partial quasi-cleavage fracture features. Subfigure (c) is dimple size distribution statistics, all showing bimodal distribution, with average dimple size decreasing with increasing annealing temperature (1.54μm for AN750-T, 1.44μm for AN800-T, 1.31μm for AN850-T). The results demonstrate that the heterogeneous multiphase structure with coarse ferrite and fine hard phases effectively mitigates stress concentration during deformation, improving ductility.

This figure presents EBSD characterization results of the necking region after tensile fracture for three Fe-0.25C-3Mn-2Al-0.4Si low-density steel samples: AN750-T (750 °C annealed + 400 °C aged), AN800-T (800 °C annealed + 400 °C aged), AN850-T (850 °C annealed + 400 °C aged). Subfigure (a) shows Kikuchi pattern quality and phase distribution maps, with retained austenite (RA, red marked) fractions of 0.018, 0.004, 0.002 for the three samples respectively, decreasing with increasing annealing temperature. Subfigure (b) displays magnified Kikuchi pattern quality and KAM maps, marking soft ferrite (F) domains, hard regions (B/TM/FM, red ellipses), and white pixel regions prone to micropore nucleation during deformation. Subfigure (c) plots point-to-point (P-T-P) orientation difference (black curves, average values 3.50°, 4.48°, 3.50° for the three samples) and Kikuchi pattern quality (red curves) along selected lines. Sharp orientation difference peaks and Kikuchi quality drops at phase boundaries confirm strong strain and dislocation accumulation at phase interfaces, supporting that heterogeneous soft-hard domain interfaces induce stress redistribution to accommodate deformation discontinuity.

This figure consists of two subplots targeting the Fe-0.25C-3Mn-2Al-0.4Si low-density steel. The first subplot is a temperature-time schematic of the thermal treatment procedure: samples are heated to 200 °C at 10 °C/s first, then heated to 980 °C at 0.01 °C/s, held at 980 °C for 300 s, and finally cooled at -30 °C/s. The second subplot shows thermal dilatometry test results, presenting dimensional change ΔL (in μm) as a function of temperature (in °C). Four critical phase transition temperatures of the steel are explicitly marked: Ac1 = 705 °C, Ac3 = 950 °C, bainite finish temperature Bf = 380 °C, and martensite start temperature Ms = 346 °C. The curves reflect dimension variations induced by thermal expansion and phase transformations during heating and cooling, which provide foundational data for heat treatment optimization to obtain the target multiphase heterogeneous microstructure for superior strength-ductility balance.

This figure presents EBSD characterization and grain size analysis of aged Fe-0.25C-3Mn-2Al low-density steel samples: AN750-T (750℃ intercritical annealing + 400℃ aging), AN800-T (800℃ intercritical annealing + 400℃ aging), AN850-T (850℃ intercritical annealing + 400℃ aging). Subfigures (a) and (b) show inverse pole figure (IPF) orientation color maps (20 μm scale bar, color coded for <001>, <101>, <111> crystal orientations) before parent grain reconstruction and after parent grain analysis, respectively. Subfigure (c) shows equivalent circle diameter distributions of deformation-induced martensite (DIM) for each sample, comparing pre-reconstruction (red curve) and post parent grain analysis (black curve) results. Measured average DIM sizes are 3.29/3.41 μm (Δ=0.12) for AN750-T, 2.94/3.09 μm (Δ=0.15) for AN800-T, 2.61/2.80 μm (Δ=0.19) for AN850-T pre/post reconstruction. The data demonstrates average DIM size decreases with increasing annealing temperature, while deviation between pre and post reconstruction values rises, supporting quantitative microstructure analysis of TRIP effect in the multiphase low-density steel.
